allies.rationale_text: Incentives explain why nominal authority can lose to the actor controlling the operational payoff, the next move, or the real fallback lane.
allies.source_quote: Power Dynamics works best in combination with models that clarify incentives

allies.rationale_text: Lock-in clarifies how integration, retraining, and dependency costs narrow the future option set and invert leverage after commitment.
allies.source_quote: Lock In explains how commitment changes the future option set

allies.rationale_text: Optionality strengthens power-dynamics analysis by preserving live fallback paths and reversible exits before leverage collapses.
allies.source_quote: preserve leverage by keeping a live fallback lane and a reversible exit path

antagonists.rationale_text: Confirmation-bias distorts power-dynamics by making teams see the leverage structure they want rather than the outside option they actually have.
antagonists.source_quote: Teams often see the leverage structure they want to see

antagonists.rationale_text: Opportunity-cost counters power-dynamics tunnel vision by asking whether winning the negotiation is even worth pursuing relative to better alternatives.
antagonists.source_quote: over-focus on winning the negotiation instead of asking whether the game is worth playing relative to better alternatives

structured_tensions.tension_text: Power-dynamics conflicts with confirmation-bias when a team keeps acting as if its outside option is stronger than the evidence supports.
structured_tensions.source_quote: A buyer keeps acting as if the supplier needs the deal more, even after capacity signals and fallback evidence say otherwise.

curation_notes.summary: The retained relation surface stays close to the source's leverage lattice: incentives, lock-in, optionality, and the explicit conflict with confirmation-bias.
donor_drops: Did not preserve broader game-theory or information-asymmetry expansion because the current vocabulary already supports a cleaner leverage surface.
open_questions: The source also names principal-agent-problem, but the kept ally set favors the more operational leverage and reversibility neighborhood.
